_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Christian County, Kentucky. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for the dark-fired tobacco capital of the Pennyroyal — formed 1797 from Logan County, with Hopkinsville as county seat. Christian County's records are well-preserved despite Civil War occupation, and the county's strong slaveholding history makes the 1850-1860 slave schedules particularly important.

previous_courthouses.location: Hopkinsville — original log courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county grew. Records transferred to successor building.

previous_courthouses.location: Hopkinsville — brick courthouse on the public square
previous_courthouses.fate: Survived the Civil War with minor damage. Union forces occupied Hopkinsville for most of the war after early Confederate presence. The courthouse was used as military headquarters at various times. Records remained largely intact.

previous_courthouses.location: Hopkinsville — second brick courthouse on the same site
previous_courthouses.fate: Served over a century. Records from this era are well-preserved. Replaced by the current 1970 building.

record_losses.cause: Civil War occupation and guerrilla activity
records_destroyed: scattered justice of the peace records
record_losses.description: Christian County experienced Civil War disruption — guerrilla raids, Confederate cavalry operations, and Union occupation — but the courthouse itself was not significantly damaged. The bound record books survived intact. Some loose papers and justice of the peace records were lost during the confusion of military occupation. The county's records are among the best-preserved in the cluster, reflecting Hopkinsville's relative stability as a Union-held garrison town for most of the war.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name}'s records are missing from the Christian County courthouse, the gap is unlikely to be caused by wartime destruction. More likely explanations include the family's pre-1797 presence in Logan County, the general incompleteness of vital records before 1911, or the family's economic circumstances (tenant farmers and laborers left fewer property records).
record_losses.source: Perrin, William Henry. County of Christian, Kentucky: Historical and Biographical. Chicago: F. A. Battey Publishing, 1884.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Christian County deed books are well-maintained and legible.
deeds.repository: Christian County Clerk, Hopkinsville; KDLA microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Kentucky land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books and estate records survive from near-formation
wills_estates.gaps: Minor — some loose estate papers from the earliest decades are incomplete. Bound will books are intact. Estate inventories are particularly detailed for slaveholding families, listing enslaved people by name after approximately 1820.

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation
marriages.gaps: Marriage bonds are more complete than minister returns before the 1850s. Some early bonds show age-related deterioration but remain legible.

court_minutes.completeness: Good — county court order books survive from formation and are relatively continuous
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in the earliest volumes. The court order books document road orders, estate settlements, apprenticeships (including apprenticeships of orphaned and freed Black children during Reconstruction), and land disputes.

vital_records.completeness: Kentucky mandated registration beginning in 1852, but compliance in Christian County was inconsistent until approximately 1911. Hopkinsville city records may be slightly more complete than outlying rural areas.
vital_records.repository: Kentucky Office of Vital Statistics, Frankfort; KDLA for pre-1911 registers

substitute_sources.description: Christian County was formed from Logan County in 1797. All records before that date are in Logan County — though the 1865 Logan courthouse fire destroyed many early records, making surviving Logan fragments especially valuable.
substitute_sources.repository: Logan County Clerk, Russellville; KDLA microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for any family in the Hopkinsville area before 1797. Because the Logan County fire destroyed many early records, Christian County's own earliest deed books (1797+) become even more critical as the primary documentation for families that moved from Logan to Christian.
substitute_sources.source: Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1925.

substitute_sources.description: Christian County was one of the heaviest slaveholding counties in Kentucky. The 1850 and 1860 slave schedules list slaveholders by name and enslaved people by age, sex, and color. Post-emancipation records include Freedmen's Bureau records (1865-1872) and the Colored Soldiers' records at the National Archives.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (slave schedules, Freedmen's Bureau); FamilySearch; Ancestry.com
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for Black genealogy in the Pennyroyal. Christian County's large enslaved population means the slave schedules are particularly rich. Freedmen's Bureau marriage registers, labor contracts, and school records document the transition to freedom.
substitute_sources.source: Freedmen's Bureau Records, National Archives, RG 105. Christian County, Kentucky.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1800-1940. Agricultural schedules (1850-1880) and slave schedules (1850-1860).
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential. The agricultural schedules document the dark-fired tobacco economy in detail — acres planted, tobacco produced, livestock owned. The slave schedules are critical for a county where enslaved people made up roughly 30-35% of the population by 1860.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Christian County, KY.

substitute_sources.source_type: Church records — Baptist, Methodist, and Cumberland Presbyterian
substitute_sources.description: Christian County's strong Cumberland Presbyterian heritage (the denomination was founded in Logan and Dickson counties, TN, just south of the Pennyroyal) makes Presbyterian church records especially important. Baptist and Methodist churches were also well-established.
substitute_sources.repository: Historical Foundation of the Cumberland Presbyterian Church, Memphis; Southern Baptist Theological Seminary Archives; individual congregations
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Cumberland Presbyterian records are particularly valuable for this region because the denomination's organizational records are well-preserved and because membership signals geographic and cultural origin in the Kentucky-Tennessee border country.
substitute_sources.source: McDonnold, B. W. History of the Cumberland Presbyterian Church. Nashville: Board of Publication of the Cumberland Presbyterian Church, 1899.

narrative_hooks: Christian County's courthouse survived the Civil War intact — {ancestor_name}'s deeds and wills are almost certainly still on the shelves in Hopkinsville, one of the best-preserved record sets in the Pennyroyal.

If {ancestor_name} enslaved people in Christian County, the estate inventories after approximately 1820 list them by name — and the Freedmen's Bureau records after 1865 document the same families making the transition to freedom.

Christian County was formed from Logan in 1797, but the Logan County courthouse fire of 1865 destroyed many of the mother county's records — making Christian County's own earliest deed books the primary evidence for families that crossed from one county to the other.

The Cumberland Presbyterian church was born in the Pennyroyal revival country, and if {ancestor_name} was Cumberland Presbyterian in Christian County, {pronoun_possessive} church records survive at the denomination's archives in Memphis — session minutes, membership rolls, and dismissal letters that track migration across the Kentucky-Tennessee border.
source_references: Perrin, William Henry. County of Christian, Kentucky: Historical and Biographical. Chicago: F. A. Battey Publishing, 1884.
Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Standard Printing Company, 1925.
McDonnold, B. W. History of the Cumberland Presbyterian Church. Nashville: Board of Publication of the Cumberland Presbyterian Church, 1899.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Kentucky County Records. Frankfort, 1937-1942.
Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Records on Microfilm. Frankfort, various editions.
